title: Do smart thermostats actually save money?
description: Smart thermostats promise savings of up to a third, but independent trials find 5 to 10 per cent. We run the honest payback maths at July 2026 cap rates.
blurb: Marketing says up to a third off heating. Trials find far less. The honest payback maths at current rates.
lede: Smart thermostat adverts make a bold pitch: spend £150 or so on a clever box and it will trim a quarter or more off your heating bill. Independent trials tell a quieter story. Most households that fit one save something, but the typical figure is closer to 5 or 10 per cent of heating energy than the 25 to 31 per cent the marketing departments quote. That gap matters, because it is the difference between a gadget that pays for itself in under two years and one that takes most of a decade. This guide runs the real numbers at current price cap rates, explains where the savings actually come from, and shows you which kind of household gets a fast payback and which would do better spending the money elsewhere.
short_answer: Yes, usually, but more modestly than the adverts suggest. Independent trials typically find smart thermostats cut heating energy by around 5 to 10 per cent, which on a typical gas-heated home is roughly £37 to £73 a year at the July 2026 cap rate of 7.33p per kWh. Against a £150 to £250 installed cost, that is a payback of about two to seven years. Households with irregular schedules, or who currently heat on a crude timer with no room thermostat, save the most and pay back fastest.

sections.h2: What a smart thermostat actually does differently
sections.html:
A basic programmable thermostat does two things: it holds the house at a set temperature, and it switches the heating on and off at fixed times. A smart thermostat keeps both of those jobs and adds layers on top:

  • Remote control. You can turn the heating off from your phone when you leave for the weekend, or warm the house up on the way home. No saving by itself, but it removes the excuse for heating an empty house.
  • Geofencing and presence detection. The system notices when everyone has left and drops the temperature automatically, then recovers before you get back. This is where most of the real-world saving lives for households with unpredictable days.
  • Learning and adaptive start. The thermostat works out how quickly your home warms and cools, so it fires the boiler at the latest moment that still hits your target temperature on time, rather than a fixed half hour early.
  • Weather compensation. Some models adjust for outside temperature and forecast, heating less aggressively on mild days.
  • Open-window detection. A sudden temperature drop pauses the heating instead of letting the boiler fight the draught.
  • Room-by-room zoning. With smart radiator valves added, you can heat the rooms you use and let the rest sit cooler, the same principle covered in our guide to radiator valves and zoning.

None of this is magic. Every one of those features saves money the same way: the boiler runs for fewer hours, or at a lower target, than it otherwise would. If your current routine is already tight, a smart thermostat has little left to trim. If your heating currently runs to a fixed schedule whether anyone is home or not, there is real fat to cut.

sections.h2: What the evidence says about real savings
sections.html:
Manufacturers quote headline figures of up to 28 or 31 per cent. Those numbers usually come from modelling that compares a smart system against a worst-case baseline: heating left on long hours at a high set point with no room thermostat at all. Almost nobody starts from there.

Independent field trials, including UK government-commissioned work on smart heating controls and academic studies that metered real homes before and after installation, consistently land much lower. Typical findings cluster around 5 to 10 per cent of space heating energy, with wide variation between households. Some homes save 15 per cent or more; a meaningful minority save nothing, usually because their old routine was already efficient or because the new app made it easier to nudge the temperature up.

That last point deserves emphasis. A thermostat you can poke from the sofa cuts both ways. The households that save are the ones that set a sensible schedule and a sensible target temperature and then leave it alone. If you are not sure what sensible looks like, start with our guide to thermostat settings, because the single biggest lever in any heating system is still the number on the dial. Each degree lower typically cuts heating energy by several per cent, and a smart thermostat cannot save you money that you give back by running the house warmer.

sections.h2: The payback maths at current prices
sections.html:
A typical UK gas-heated home uses around 11,500 kWh of gas a year, the figure Ofgem uses as its standard for a medium household. Roughly 10,000 kWh of that goes on space heating, with the rest on hot water and cooking. At the current cap rate of 7.33p per kWh, that space heating costs about £733 a year.

Smart thermostat kits for a combi boiler typically cost £120 to £220 for the hardware. Professional installation adds £50 to £100 if you do not fit it yourself, though many kits are a straightforward DIY swap on a standard backplate. The table below shows what different saving rates are worth and how long each takes to repay a £150 self-install or a £250 professional install.

Heating savingGas saved per yearMoney saved per yearPayback on £150 (DIY)Payback on £250 (installed)
5% (cautious)500 kWh£374.1 years6.8 years
8% (typical trial result)800 kWh£592.5 years4.2 years
10% (good result)1,000 kWh£732.1 years3.4 years
15% (irregular schedule, poor old controls)1,500 kWh£1101.4 years2.3 years

At 7.33p per kWh for gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Assumes roughly 10,000 kWh of the Ofgem typical 11,500 kWh annual gas use goes on space heating.

Two honest caveats. First, larger or older homes spend far more than £733 a year on heating, so the same percentage saving is worth proportionally more and the payback shortens. Our guide to the cost of heating a house in the UK shows how widely that baseline varies. Second, smart radiator valves for whole-house zoning cost roughly £50 to £80 per radiator on top, which can push a full system past £500. Zoning savings are real, but at that spend the payback stretches well beyond the figures above unless your heating bill is large.

sections.h2: Who pays back fastest, and who should not bother
sections.html:
The payback case is strongest when at least one of these applies:

  • Irregular hours. Shift work, hybrid working with unpredictable office days, frequent nights away. Geofencing turns every unplanned absence into a saving that a fixed timer would have missed.
  • Crude existing controls. If you have a boiler timer but no room thermostat, or a single ancient dial in the hallway, you are heating to a guess. Any proper thermostatic control will help, and a smart one helps most.
  • A large heating bill. A 1930s four-bed semi spending £1,400 a year on heating gets twice the cash saving of the typical home at the same percentage, so the device repays in roughly half the time.
  • Rooms with very different use. A spare room, home office or guest bedroom that only needs heat sometimes makes zoning genuinely useful.

The case is weak when the opposite holds. A small, well-insulated flat with a £400 heating bill and a regular nine-to-five routine might save £20 to £40 a year, and a decent £25 programmable room thermostat captures most of that. Renters should also pause: some kits move with you easily, but anything wired in stays with the property unless your landlord agrees otherwise.

It is also worth being clear about what a smart thermostat does not fix. It cannot make a poorly insulated house cheap to heat, and it saves less per pound spent than basics like loft insulation or draught-proofing in a leaky home. Treat it as a controls upgrade that sits on top of the fundamentals covered across our heating guides, not a substitute for them.

sections.h2: The hidden bonus: modulation and lower flow temperatures
sections.html:
There is a second saving channel that rarely makes the adverts, and for some homes it is the bigger one. Most basic thermostats control the boiler with a simple on-off relay: full blast, then nothing. Many smart thermostats can instead talk to a compatible boiler over a modulating protocol such as OpenTherm, asking for exactly as much heat as the house needs at that moment.

A modulating setup keeps the boiler running low and steady at a reduced flow temperature, which keeps a condensing boiler in its efficient condensing zone for far more of the time. That alone is typically worth several per cent on gas use, on top of any scheduling savings, and it is the same physics behind our guide to boiler flow temperature. If your boiler supports OpenTherm or the manufacturer's own modulating bus, choosing a thermostat that speaks it is one of the most worthwhile boxes to tick on the spec sheet.

The same logic applies even more strongly to heat pumps, which live or die by low flow temperatures and steady running. Most heat pumps ship with their own weather-compensating controller, and bolting a third-party smart thermostat on top can actually hurt efficiency by forcing stop-start behaviour. If you are heading that way, read up on how heat pumps work before buying any controls, and let the heat pump's native controller lead.

Smart thermostats do save money for most households, just not the headline numbers. Plan on 5 to 10 per cent of your heating bill, which at current cap rates means roughly £37 to £73 a year for a typical gas-heated home and proportionally more for a big or draughty one. A self-installed kit at around £150 generally repays in two to four years and then keeps saving for the life of the device, which is a solid if unspectacular return.

Buy one if your schedule is irregular, your current controls are poor, your heating bill is large, or your boiler supports OpenTherm modulation. Skip it, or buy a cheap programmable thermostat instead, if you live in a small efficient home with a fixed routine and a low bill. And whatever you fit, remember that the set point still does the heavy lifting: a smart thermostat at 21°C costs more to run than a dumb one at 19°C. Get the basics right first with our guide to what temperature to set your thermostat, then let the clever box defend that choice automatically.

faq.q: How much does a smart thermostat save per year in the UK?
faq.a: Independent trials typically find savings of 5 to 10 per cent of space heating energy. For a typical gas-heated home using around 10,000 kWh a year for heating, that is roughly £37 to £73 a year at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 7.33p per kWh. Larger homes with bigger bills save proportionally more.

faq.q: How long does a smart thermostat take to pay for itself?
faq.a: A self-installed kit costing around £150 typically pays back in two to four years at a realistic saving rate. A professionally installed system at around £250 takes three to seven years. Homes with irregular schedules or very poor existing controls can pay back in under two years.

faq.q: Are the savings claims of 25 to 31 per cent realistic?
faq.a: Generally not. Those figures come from modelling against a worst-case baseline with no room thermostat and long heating hours. Real metered trials of homes that already had basic controls find savings closer to 5 to 10 per cent, with some homes saving nothing at all.

faq.q: Is a smart thermostat better than a programmable thermostat?
faq.a: It saves more if your routine is irregular, because geofencing catches absences a fixed timer misses, and OpenTherm models can run the boiler more efficiently. If you keep a regular schedule, a £25 programmable room thermostat set sensibly captures most of the same saving for a sixth of the price.

faq.q: Do smart thermostats work with heat pumps?
faq.a: Some do, but heat pumps usually run most efficiently on their own weather-compensating controller, which keeps flow temperatures low and running steady. Adding a third-party smart thermostat can force inefficient stop-start operation, so check with your installer before replacing a heat pump's native controls.
